meta.name: TCL Tech Solutions — Upgrade Membership Module
meta.purpose: Integration specification to port the standalone deal-modeling tool into the production TCL platform while reproducing the source design system EXACTLY: identical font, spacing, formatting, and motion.
meta.owner: Damon Jackson, CEO, TCL Tech Solutions
meta.fidelityContract: Implementers MUST reproduce every token below verbatim. Do not substitute a design-system equivalent (e.g. a framework's default font or spacing scale). Pixel values, hex colors, easing curves and durations are normative, not advisory.

integrationSteps: 1. Mount the module at a route such as /tools/upgrade-model. It is fully client-side; no backend is required for the calculator itself.
2. Reproduce the design system in 'designSystem' as CSS custom properties on the module root. Copy the :root variable block verbatim.
3. Rebuild each component in 'components' to the exact paddings, radii, font sizes and colors given. Use the source HTML as the reference DOM.

4. Implement 'motion' exactly: only the toast uses a CSS transition (opacity 0.25s ease); hover state changes are instantaneous (no transition declared); bars render at a fixed computed width with no width animation. Match this precisely.
5. Wire the 'calculationEngine' functions exactly as specified so economics are identical to the spreadsheet model.
6. Seed state from 'dataModel.defaults'. Persist edits via the host platform store, or retain the source's safe localStorage('tcl_model') + JSON import/export fallback.
7. Keep the eight-tab information architecture in 'navigation' (seven working tabs + Glossary). Default landing tab = 'dashboard'.

8. Reproduce the jargon-explainer system in 'jargonExplainers': every specialized term is wrapped with a hover/focus/tap tooltip and the full 'glossary' is its own tab. Implement the tooltip component and motion exactly as specified; wire each term/info icon to its glossary key.
9. Validate against 'acceptanceTests' — all must pass before release.

borders.kpiLeftAccent: 4px solid var(--steel)  (green/amber variants swap the accent color)

layout.grid2: two columns 1fr 1fr, gap 18px; collapses to a single column at max-width 820px

header.logo: 42x42px, radius 8px, white bg, navy text, bold 18px, letter-spacing -1px
header.buttons: rgba(255,255,255,.14) bg, 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.35), padding 7px 12px, radius 6px, 12px; hover bg rgba(255,255,255,.28)

nav.button: color #c7d3e8, padding 13px 16px, 13px; hover color #fff; active color #fff + bold + 3px #7aa6e8 bottom border

input.nameVariant: full width, left align, ink color, normal weight

brow.total: bg #f0f5ff, 15px, radius 6px, padding 11px 8px, marginTop 4px, value navy

bar.label: 90px width, 12px muted
bar.fill: barFillGradient, right-aligned white 11px bold label, min-width 34px, computed width %

buttons.addbtn: var(--steel) bg #fff, radius 6px, padding 8px 14px, 13px; hover var(--navy)
buttons.rowbtn: transparent, 1px solid #c3cee0, muted, padding 3px 8px, 12px; hover red text+border, bg #f6f9fe

term.desc: Inline jargon word that reveals a tooltip
term.style: border-bottom 1px dotted #2e5496; cursor help; hover/focus bg #eef5ff
term.trigger: mouseover / focusin show, click navigates to its Glossary entry

info.desc: Small circular i icon placed after a label
info.style: 15x15px circle, bg #2e5496, white italic bold 'i' in Georgia serif, radius 50%, marginLeft 5px; hover bg #1f3864

tooltip.desc: Single floating bubble reused for all terms
tooltip.style: fixed, z-index 100, max-width 300px, bg #10233f, #fff, 12.5px, line-height 1.5, padding 10px 12px, radius 8px, shadow 0 6px 22px rgba(10,25,50,.32)
tooltip.title: .tt = bold #9ec5ff 12px block
tooltip.footer: .more = #7aa6e8 11px 'Tap for the full entry in the Glossary'
tooltip.arrow: 9-11px square rotated 45deg, bg matches bubble, repositions top/bottom via .below
tooltip.positioning: centered above the term; flips below if it would clip the top; clamped 8px from viewport edges; arrow tracks term center via --ax custom property

glossary.context: .gloss-ctx 12px var(--muted), label 'On this platform:' in var(--steel) bold

motion.principle: The source is deliberately restrained. Reproduce EXACTLY — do not add framework animations.

transitions.position: fixed; bottom 20px; left 50%; translateX(-50%); z-index 50; pointer-events none

transitions.trigger: shown on hover/focus of any [data-term]; hidden on mouseout/focusout/scroll
transitions.note: Reuses the SAME 0.25s ease timing as the toast for a consistent feel. This is the only motion added beyond the original source.

instantaneousStateChanges: header buttons hover (background only, NO transition declared)
nav buttons hover (color only, NO transition)
noAnimation: bar-fill widths render statically at their computed percentage (no width tween)
tab switching is an instant display:none/block swap (no fade or slide)
no scroll, parallax, or entrance animations anywhere
motion.reducedMotion: The only animated element (toast) is non-essential; honor prefers-reduced-motion by setting its transition to none if the host platform enforces it.

navigation.switchBehavior: Set .tab.active on target, .active on its nav button; instant display swap; re-render all views.

jargonExplainers.intent: No user should hit a word they do not understand. Every specialized term is explained inline (tooltip) and in full (Glossary tab), written at an explain-like-I'm-5 reading level.

mechanics.inlineTerm: Wrap the word in <span class='term' data-term='KEY' tabindex='0'>. Dotted underline signals it is explainable.
mechanics.infoIcon: Place <span class='info' data-term='KEY' tabindex='0'> (a small 'i') after a label that has no natural word to underline.
mechanics.tooltip: A single reused #tip bubble shows GLOSSARY[KEY].term (title) + .short (the one-breath explanation) + a 'Tap for the full entry' footer.
mechanics.click: Clicking any term or info icon navigates to the Glossary tab and highlights that entry (bg #fff7da for 1.6s).
mechanics.glossaryTab: Lists every term with: bold title, the plain-English 'eli5' line, and an 'On this platform:' context line. Includes a live search filter.

jargonExplainers.coverage: All 30 terms below are wrapped at least once in the UI and/or carry an info icon, and all appear in the Glossary tab. Implementers MUST preserve this coverage when restyling.
authoringRules: eli5 = one sentence a newcomer could read aloud in a single breath; no jargon inside the jargon definition.
onThisPlatform = how the term behaves specifically in THIS tool, not a textbook definition.
Never define a term using another undefined term. If you must, that second term also gets an entry.

msrp.eli5: The full price the maker says it should sell for — the big number before any deal.
msrp.onThisPlatform: On this platform it's the 'before' price we measure every discount against.

dealer.eli5: What TCL actually pays its supplier to buy the item. Always lower than the sticker price.
dealer.onThisPlatform: This is TCL's floor: we can never sell below it without losing money.

spread.eli5: The gap between what TCL pays (dealer cost) and the sticker price (MSRP). It's the wiggle room to give a discount and still profit.
spread.onThisPlatform: The single most important number here: the customer's credit can never be bigger than the spread.

points.eli5: The spread written as a percentage of the sticker price. '50 points' means the gap is half the price.
points.onThisPlatform: Bigger points = more room to make money. 50+ points is what makes a component great for this program.

credit.eli5: Money the customer slowly builds up by paying monthly, which they later knock off the price of a new component.
credit.onThisPlatform: Think of it like a gift card that fills up over time — but capped so it can't grow forever.

bonus.eli5: Extra credit TCL adds on top of what the customer paid in, as a thank-you for joining.
bonus.onThisPlatform: Example: a member pays in $480 over a year but their credit is $600 — that extra $120 is the bonus.

subscription.eli5: The set amount the customer pays every month just to be a member of the club.
subscription.onThisPlatform: This steady monthly money is the heart of how the program profits.

tier.eli5: A membership level. Higher tiers cost more per month but build bigger credits.
tier.onThisPlatform: Standard, Plus, and Premium are the tiers — plus a Programming tier for labor.

oop.eli5: The cash the customer actually hands over at the counter, AFTER their credit is subtracted.
oop.onThisPlatform: Sticker price minus credit = out-of-pocket. It can never drop below TCL's dealer cost.

savings.eli5: What the customer truly saves once you subtract the monthly fees they already paid.
savings.onThisPlatform: A neat fact: this always equals (credit − subscription paid), no matter which item they pick.

hardware_margin.eli5: The profit on the gear itself: what the customer pays for it minus what TCL paid the supplier.
hardware_margin.onThisPlatform: Separate from the subscription money. On thin-spread items this can shrink to near zero.

margin.eli5: What slice of the money coming in is actually profit after costs.
margin.onThisPlatform: Higher is better. Shown as a percentage of the subscription revenue.

net_profit.eli5: What's left over after every cost is paid — the real bottom line.
net_profit.onThisPlatform: Subscription money + hardware margin − servicing costs.

float.eli5: All the monthly money piling up in TCL's account from members who haven't upgraded yet. A big, steady pool of cash.
float.onThisPlatform: Because most members upgrade rarely, this pool keeps growing and is where most of the profit lives.

volume.eli5: Simply having LOTS of customers. Small money per person becomes big money across many people.
volume.onThisPlatform: The program needs many members for the math to work — a few won't move the needle.

float_volume_engine.eli5: The way the business makes money: collect small monthly fees from MANY members (volume), most of whom upgrade rarely, so the steady pool of fees (float) earns far more than the occasional discount costs.
float_volume_engine.onThisPlatform: It's the exact same trick AT&T uses with phones: lots of subscribers, a big cash pool, and only the occasional payout.

redemption.eli5: When a customer finally cashes in their built-up credit to get a component.
redemption.onThisPlatform: Each redemption costs TCL a little discount but the member keeps paying their monthly fee.

redemption_freq.eli5: How often the average member actually upgrades — like 'once every 3 years.'
redemption_freq.onThisPlatform: The LESS often they upgrade, the more the float grows. The program is built assuming infrequent upgrades.

rollover.eli5: If a member doesn't spend their credit this year, part of it carries over into next year.
rollover.onThisPlatform: Keeps members feeling rewarded for staying — without handing back every dollar.

rollover_cap.eli5: A ceiling on how high unused credit can stack up, so a member can't save for years and then wipe out TCL's profit in one purchase.
rollover_cap.onThisPlatform: Here it's set to 2× the first-year credit — the key defense against 'credit banking'.

rec_credit.eli5: A safe credit amount (75% of the spread) that still feels like a deal but leaves profit for TCL.
rec_credit.onThisPlatform: A guardrail so you don't accidentally give away the entire spread.

fit.eli5: A simple grade — STRONG, MARGINAL, or POOR — for whether a component is worth building the program around.
fit.onThisPlatform: Based on points: 50%+ = STRONG, 35–50% = MARGINAL, under 35% = POOR.

viable.eli5: Viable = the deal makes money for TCL AND saves the customer money. CHECK = something's off and needs a second look.
viable.onThisPlatform: 'CHECK' appears when TCL would lose money or the customer would save nothing.

basis.eli5: Whether the price is a real, confirmed number or a placeholder guess to be replaced.
basis.onThisPlatform: 'Estimate' rows use stand-in pricing — overwrite them with TCL's real dealer costs.

servicing.eli5: What it costs TCL each year to support a member — visits, tweaks, programming time.
servicing.onThisPlatform: Subtracted from revenue to get true net profit.

portfolio.eli5: The whole group of members looked at together, instead of one customer at a time.
portfolio.onThisPlatform: This view answers: across ALL members, does the program make money?

installment.eli5: Paying for something in small monthly pieces instead of all at once.
installment.onThisPlatform: How AT&T spreads a phone's cost over 36 months.

tradein.eli5: Handing back your old device to lower the cost of the new one.
tradein.onThisPlatform: AT&T requires this; TCL's credit model does NOT — a point in TCL's favor.

anchoring.eli5: Showing the big sticker price first so the discount feels large by comparison.
anchoring.onThisPlatform: Why marketing should always quote MSRP, not TCL's everyday street price.

programming.eli5: The labor of setting up and configuring the smart-home system, charged by the hour.
programming.onThisPlatform: The $25/mo Programming tier covers a couple of hours of this each year.

calculationEngine.note: Reproduce verbatim. These match TCL_Upgrade_Membership_Model.xlsx exactly. Currency rounded for display only; compute on raw values.

deal.creditApplied: min(tierCredit(tier), spread(component))   // GOVERNING RULE: never exceeds spread
deal.savingsVsMSRP: component.msrp - customerTotal   // identity: equals creditApplied - annualSub
deal.viable: customerOOP >= component.dealer AND savingsVsMSRP > 0   // else flag 'CHECK'

formatting.money: round to integer; negatives in parentheses; null/NaN -> em dash
formatting.number: locale grouped, max decimals as specified per field

acceptanceTests.expect: savingsVsMSRP == tierCredit - annualSub for any component where creditApplied is not spread-capped
Every key in 'glossary' resolves; no [data-term] in the UI points to a missing key; Glossary tab renders all 30 entries with a working search filter.
Hover/focus of any term or info icon shows #tip with that term's eli5; click navigates to the Glossary entry and highlights it.

assets.role: Reference implementation + design source of truth (single self-contained file).
Authoritative economics; the calculationEngine must match this workbook (7 sheets, 320 formulas).
This file — normative design + logic contract.
